name: Solid Shapes Around Us — Roll, Slide and Stack
whatIsIt: Flat shapes live on paper, but real things are SOLID — you can hold them. A ball is a SPHERE (it rolls everywhere). A box or dice is a CUBE (it has 6 flat faces, and it stacks). A tin can is a CYLINDER (flat on top and bottom, round in the middle — it rolls AND stacks). An ice-cream cone is a CONE (round bottom, one point). Solids have FACES (flat sides), EDGES (where faces meet) and CORNERS (points).
whyNeeded: Everything a child touches is a solid shape — balls, boxes, cans, dice, party hats. Knowing which shapes roll, slide, and stack helps in building blocks, packing, and understanding the world before moving on to their flat drawings.
realLifeUses: A ball rolls because it is a sphere
Boxes stack neatly because they are cubes
A tin can rolls on its side but stacks on its ends
An ice-cream cone and a party hat are cones

vocabulary.meaning: A perfectly round solid like a ball — it rolls and has no corners.
A box shape with 6 equal flat square faces, like a dice.
A can shape — two flat round ends and a curved side.
A face is a flat side; an edge is where two faces meet; a corner is a point.

story.title: Anu's Toy Basket
story.text: Anu tips out her toy basket: a ball, a dice, and a tin of biscuits. 'Which ones roll?' asks Dadi. Anu pushes them: the ball rolls away, the tin rolls on its side, but the dice just slides and stops. 'The dice has flat faces — I can count them!' She touches each side: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. 'Six faces!' Dadi claps. 'That's a cube.'
story.extractedProblem: How many flat faces does a dice (a cube) have?
story.answerInStory: 6 — Anu counts six flat square faces on the cube.

visual.caption: Real 3D solids you can spin: a sphere rolls, a cube stacks (6 faces), a cylinder does both, a cone has one point.

teachingGallery.title: Roll it or stack it?
teachingGallery.note: Round parts roll; flat faces let a shape stack and slide.

examples.caption: A ball (sphere) has no flat face, so it rolls anywhere.
A dice (cube) has 6 flat faces, 8 corners and 12 edges — it stacks.
A tin can (cylinder) rolls on its side but stacks on its flat ends.

standardMethod.summary: Pick up the solid. Feel for flat faces and round parts. Round parts roll; flat faces stack and slide. Count faces, corners and edges by touching each one.
steps: Hold the object and look for FLAT faces and ROUND parts.
If it is all round (a ball), it is a sphere and it rolls.
If it has flat square faces (a dice), it is a cube — count the 6 faces.
A can (cylinder) has round ends and rolls on its side; a cone has one point.
Test it: does it roll, slide, or stack?

workedExamples.problem: How many flat faces does a cube (dice) have?
steps: Touch each flat side and count.
Top, bottom, and the four around = 1,2,3,4,5,6.

workedExamples.problem: Does a ball (sphere) roll or stack?
steps: A ball is round all over with no flat face.
With no flat face it cannot sit still to stack.

commonMistakes.mistake: Calling a ball a 'circle' or a dice a 'square' — mixing flat shapes with solids.
commonMistakes.fix: A circle is FLAT (on paper); a ball is a SOLID you can hold (a sphere). A square is flat; a dice is a cube.

commonMistakes.mistake: Saying a cube has 4 faces (forgetting the top and bottom).
commonMistakes.fix: Count ALL sides: 4 around PLUS the top and bottom = 6 faces.

easy.q: How many flat faces does a cube (dice) have?

medium.q: How many corners (points) does a cube have?
hintLadder: Count the pointy corners of a dice.

options.label: Cylinder — rolls on its side, stacks on its ends

medium.answer: Cylinder — rolls on its side, stacks on its ends

challenge.q: How many edges (where two faces meet) does a cube have?
hintLadder: Count the lines along a dice.
4 on top, 4 on the bottom, 4 standing up.

challenge.q: Riya calls a ball a 'circle'. What's the fix?

options.label: A ball is a solid SPHERE; a circle is flat on paper
Ball and circle are the same

challenge.answer: A ball is a solid SPHERE; a circle is flat on paper
hintLadder: A circle is flat; a ball is solid.

options.label: A box (cube) — it has flat faces

questions.answer: A box (cube) — it has flat faces

options.label: Cone — round bottom and one point

questions.answer: Cone — round bottom and one point

teachBackPrompt: Find a ball, a box and a tin at home. Show a grown-up which one rolls, which stacks, and which does both. Count the 6 faces of the box out loud, and name each solid: sphere, cube, cylinder.

revisionCard.summary: Solids are shapes you can hold. Sphere (ball) rolls, no corners. Cube (dice/box) has 6 flat faces, 8 corners, 12 edges — it stacks. Cylinder (can) rolls on its side, stacks on its ends. Cone has a round bottom and one point. Flat shapes live on paper; solids live in your hands.

rememberIt.hook: Solid shapes you can hold: cube, sphere, cylinder, cone
rememberIt.unpack: Ball = sphere (rolls), box = cuboid, tin = cylinder, party hat = cone. Notice flat faces vs curved.

studentQuestions.q: How do I tell a cube from a sphere?
studentQuestions.a: A cube has flat square faces and corners; a sphere is round all over and rolls.
Sphere, cylinder, and cone - they have curved surfaces. A cube will not roll.
